code: scenarios <- scn$scenarios seeds     <- c(101, 102, 103)  power_tab <- data.frame(   Scenario = character(0), LogRank = numeric(0), RMST = numeric(0),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) examples <- vector("list", length(scenarios)) names(examples) <- names(scenarios)  for (i in seq_along(scenarios)) {   s   <- scenarios[[i]]   dat <- do.call(     simdata_fast,     c(s$args, list(nsim = nsim, d.hazard = eta, seed = seeds[i]))   )   res <- analysis_fast(     dat, control = 1,     time.looks = study_dur,     stat = c("logrank", "rmst"),     tau = tau, side = 1   )   s_lr   <- simsummary_fast(res, p.col = "logrank.p", alpha = alpha)   s_rmst <- simsummary_fast(res, p.col = "rmst.p",    alpha = alpha)   power_tab <- rbind(power_tab, data.frame(     Scenario = s$label,     LogRank  = s_lr[s_lr$look   == "overall", "cum.reject"],     RMST     = s_rmst[s_rmst$look == "overall", "cum.reject"],     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ))   examples[[i]] <- dat[dat$sim == 1L, c("tte", "event", "group")] } ex  <- examples[["Crossing"]] fit <- kmcurve_fast(ex$tte, ex$event, ex$group, control = 1) plot(fit, hr = TRUE, rmst = TRUE, tau = tau, bw = 3)

title: Group-sequential design with correlated PFS and OS under the Fleischer model
text: subject group g Fleischer maximal-independence model (Fleischer, Gaschler-Markefski, Bluhmki, 2009) takes independent time--progression TTP ~ Exp(lam1) overall survival time OS ~ Exp(lam2), sets Since TTP OS independent exponentials, PFS exponential hazard Lambda = lam1 + lam2. Given medians PFS OS, OS hazard lam2 = log(2) / median_OS PFS hazard Lambda = log(2) / median_PFS, implied TTP hazard lam1 = Lambda - lam2, must positive (median OS least median PFS group). dependence two endpoints induced entirely shared OS component.

title: Group-sequential design with correlated PFS and OS under the Fleischer model
text: PFS hazard ratio stronger effect OS hazard ratio modest, usual pattern treatment delays progression extends survival.
code: nsim <- 5000 seed <- 20260611  # Per-group sample sizes (group 1 = control, group 2 = treatment) n_control <- 300 n_treat   <- 300 r_alloc   <- n_treat / n_control          # allocation ratio treatment:control  # Piecewise-uniform accrual: 20 per month, then 40 per month (600 enrolled by month 18) a_time <- c(0, 6) a_rate <- c(20, 40)  # Median PFS and OS by group, in months mst_PFS_C <- 6 mst_PFS_T <- 9 mst_OS_C  <- 14 mst_OS_T  <- 18  # Exponential hazards implied by the medians lam_PFS_C <- log(2) / mst_PFS_C lam_PFS_T <- log(2) / mst_PFS_T lam_OS_C  <- log(2) / mst_OS_C lam_OS_T  <- log(2) / mst_OS_T  # Fleischer maximal-independence: PFS hazard = TTP hazard + OS hazard, # so the latent TTP hazard is the PFS hazard minus the OS hazard. lam_TTP_C <- lam_PFS_C - lam_OS_C lam_TTP_T <- lam_PFS_T - lam_OS_T stopifnot(lam_TTP_C > 0, lam_TTP_T > 0)  # Non-informative exponential dropout, 10 percent per year on the month scale eta <- -log(1 - 0.10) / 12  # Event-driven looks (cumulative PFS event counts): #   look 1 = futility, look 2 = efficacy interim, look 3 = efficacy final d_PFS <- c(200, 300, 400) L <- length(d_PFS)  # One-sided overall significance level alpha_total <- 0.025  c(HR_PFS = lam_PFS_T / lam_PFS_C, HR_OS = lam_OS_T / lam_OS_C) #>    HR_PFS     HR_OS  #> 0.6666667 0.7777778

title: Group-sequential design with correlated PFS and OS under the Fleischer model
text: single simdata_fast call generates two correlated endpoints directly illness-death structure. intermediate event (progression) hazard h01 = lam_TTP terminal event (death) hazard h02 = lam_OS; post-progression death hazard left default, equal h02, reproduces maximal-independence model described . first endpoint e1 PFS terminal endpoint e2 OS. dropout time shared two endpoints, subject drops censored PFS OS time.
code: # A single call returns both correlated endpoints. e1 is PFS (the state-0 exit: # progression or death), e2 is OS (the terminal event), and dropout_time is the # shared non-informative dropout. The observed columns e1_tte / e1_event and # e2_tte / e2_event are each already censored at the shared dropout time. df <- simdata_fast(   nsim       = nsim,   n          = c(n_control, n_treat),   a.time     = a_time,   a.rate     = a_rate,   h01.hazard = list(lam_TTP_C, lam_TTP_T),   h02.hazard = list(lam_OS_C, lam_OS_T),   d.hazard   = eta,   seed       = seed )  # Observed PFS endpoint (e1). pfs_dat <- data.frame(   sim          = df$sim,   group        = df$group,   accrual_time = df$accrual_time,   tte          = df$e1_tte,   event        = df$e1_event )  # Observed OS endpoint (e2). os_dat <- data.frame(   sim          = df$sim,   group        = df$group,   accrual_time = df$accrual_time,   os_tte       = df$e2_tte,   os_event     = df$e2_event )

title: Group-sequential design with correlated PFS and OS under the Fleischer model

code: pfs_res <- analysis_fast(   pfs_dat,   control     = 1,   event.looks = d_PFS,   stat        = "logrank",   side        = 1 )  # Per-simulation calendar cutoffs A_l (rows = simulation, columns = look) A_mat <- matrix(pfs_res$cutoff, nrow = nsim, ncol = L, byrow = TRUE)  # With this sample size and these event targets the looks are reached in # essentially every simulation; warn if any target is not reached. if (!all(pfs_res$reached)) {   warning("Some PFS event targets were not reached; increase n or lower d_PFS.") }  # Standardized PFS log-rank statistics (natural sign: benefit is negative) Z_PFS <- matrix(pfs_res$logrank.z, nrow = nsim, ncol = L, byrow = TRUE)

PFS reaches event targets construction, OS, slower endpoint, accrues far fewer events analysis times.
code: big <- max(A_mat, na.rm = TRUE) + 1  os_blocks <- vector("list", L) for (l in seq_len(L)) {   A_l    <- A_mat[os_dat$sim, l]                        # per-row cutoff at look l   follow <- pmax(0, A_l - os_dat$accrual_time)          # administrative follow-up   obs_t  <- pmin(os_dat$os_tte, follow)                 # observed OS time at the look   obs_e  <- os_dat$os_event *     as.integer(os_dat$accrual_time + os_dat$os_tte <= A_l)    cut_l <- data.frame(     sim          = os_dat$sim,     group        = os_dat$group,     accrual_time = 0,     tte          = obs_t,     event        = obs_e   )   res_l <- analysis_fast(     cut_l, control = 1, time.looks = big, stat = "logrank", side = 1   )    # Relabel as look l and report the PFS-driven cutoff for the timing summary;   # keep only the columns that are meaningful for the censored OS data.   res_l$look       <- l   res_l$look.value <- d_PFS[l]   res_l$cutoff     <- A_mat[, l]   os_blocks[[l]] <- res_l[, c("sim", "look", "look.value", "cutoff",                               "n.event", "logrank.z", "logrank.chisq",                               "logrank.p")] }  os_res <- do.call(rbind, os_blocks) os_res <- os_res[order(os_res$sim, os_res$look), ]  # Standardized OS log-rank statistics Z_OS <- matrix(NA_real_, nsim, L) for (l in seq_len(L)) Z_OS[, l] <- os_blocks[[l]]$logrank.z d_PFS_emp <- colMeans(matrix(pfs_res$n.event, nrow = nsim, ncol = L, byrow = TRUE)) d_OS_emp  <- colMeans(matrix(os_res$n.event,  nrow = nsim, ncol = L, byrow = TRUE)) A_mean    <- colMeans(A_mat)  data.frame(   look          = seq_len(L),   d_PFS_target  = d_PFS,   d_PFS_mean    = round(d_PFS_emp),   d_OS_mean     = round(d_OS_emp),   cutoff_months = round(A_mean, 1) ) #>   look d_PFS_target d_PFS_mean d_OS_mean cutoff_months #> 1    1          200        200       112          15.3 #> 2    2          300        300       175          19.0 #> 3    3          400        400       250          24.0

Two-look Lan-DeMets alpha-spending boundaries computed gsDesign information fractions implied event counts: PFS information fractions exact event-target ratios, OS information fractions use mean OS event counts. gsDesign reports boundaries convention positive Z favors treatment, negated match natural sign logrank.z, treatment benefit negative value. first look carries non-binding futility rule PFS, expressed threshold standardized statistic.
code: timing_PFS_eff <- c(d_PFS[2] / d_PFS[3], 1) timing_OS_eff  <- c(d_OS_emp[2] / d_OS_emp[3], 1)  gs_PFS <- gsDesign::gsDesign(   k = 2, test.type = 1, alpha = alpha_total,   timing = timing_PFS_eff, sfu = gsDesign::sfLDOF ) gs_OS <- gsDesign::gsDesign(   k = 2, test.type = 1, alpha = alpha_total,   timing = timing_OS_eff, sfu = gsDesign::sfLDPocock )  b_PFS <- gs_PFS$upper$bound      # positive z-boundaries (interim, final) b_OS  <- gs_OS$upper$bound  # Efficacy boundaries on the natural-sign scale; NA at the futility-only look 1. eff_PFS <- c(NA, -b_PFS[1], -b_PFS[2]) eff_OS  <- c(NA, -b_OS[1],  -b_OS[2])  # Non-binding PFS futility at look 1: stop if the observed PFS hazard ratio is at # or above futility_HR. On the natural-sign scale this is logrank.z at or above # log(futility_HR) * sqrt(r * d) / (1 + r). futility_HR <- 1.0 fut1 <- log(futility_HR) * sqrt(r_alloc * d_PFS[1]) / (1 + r_alloc) fut_PFS <- c(fut1, NA, NA)  data.frame(   look        = seq_len(L),   PFS_efficacy = round(eff_PFS, 3),   PFS_futility = round(fut_PFS, 3),   OS_efficacy  = round(eff_OS, 3) ) #>   look PFS_efficacy PFS_futility OS_efficacy #> 1    1           NA            0          NA #> 2    2       -2.340           NA      -2.060 #> 3    3       -2.012           NA      -2.252

text: procedure tests OS PFS declared significant, OS may claimed look , later look , PFS claim. per-simulation rejection looks recovered boundary-crossing logic, joint power probability declaring endpoints gatekeeping rule. marginal OS power probability crossing OS efficacy boundary ignoring gatekeeping, hierarchical OS power probability claiming OS within procedure. latter exceed PFS power, since OS reachable PFS rejection.
code: # First efficacy-crossing look for a matrix of statistics, honouring an optional # futility rule that stops the trial without a rejection. reject_look <- function(Z, efficacy, futility = NULL) {   nl   <- ncol(Z)   ns   <- nrow(Z)   stop_flag <- logical(ns)   out  <- rep(NA_integer_, ns)   for (l in seq_len(nl)) {     active <- !stop_flag     if (!is.na(efficacy[l])) {       hit <- active & !is.na(Z[, l]) & Z[, l] <= efficacy[l]       out[hit] <- l       stop_flag[hit] <- TRUE     }     if (!is.null(futility) && !is.na(futility[l])) {       fut_hit <- !stop_flag & active & !is.na(Z[, l]) & Z[, l] >= futility[l]       stop_flag[fut_hit] <- TRUE     }   }   out }  pfs_look <- reject_look(Z_PFS, eff_PFS, fut_PFS) os_look  <- reject_look(Z_OS,  eff_OS)  pfs_reject <- !is.na(pfs_look) os_reject  <- !is.na(os_look)  # Hierarchical gatekeeping: OS is claimed only if PFS is claimed at the same or # an earlier look. joint_reject <- pfs_reject & os_reject & (os_look >= pfs_look)  power_table <- data.frame(   quantity = c("PFS power (marginal)",                "OS power (marginal)",                "OS power (after PFS, hierarchical)",                "Joint power (both endpoints)"),   value = c(mean(pfs_reject),             mean(os_reject),             mean(joint_reject),             mean(joint_reject)) ) power_table$value <- round(power_table$value, 3) power_table #>                             quantity value #> 1               PFS power (marginal) 0.977 #> 2                OS power (marginal) 0.460 #> 3 OS power (after PFS, hierarchical) 0.452 #> 4       Joint power (both endpoints) 0.452

title: Introduction to FastSurvival
text: package two families functions. estimation testing functions operate single dataset return S3 object print() method. survfit_fast() evaluates Kaplan-Meier estimate single time point. survdiff_fast() computes log-rank test weighted stratified variants. coxph_fast() returns closed-form hazard ratio. rmst_fast() returns restricted mean survival time, wmst_fast() window mean survival time interval. milestone_fast() compares survival milestone timepoint, medsurv_fast() compares median survival times. maxcombo_fast() computes max-combo test, rmw_fast() robust modestly-weighted log-rank test, wkm_fast() weighted Kaplan-Meier (Pepe-Fleming) test. ahsw_fast() computes average hazard survival weight, ahr_fast() Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio. simulation functions support full simulation study. simdata_fast() generates individual patient data one- two-group trials. analysis_fast() performs interim sequential analyses simulated data one looks, can compute estimation testing statistics , optionally within subgroups. simsummary_fast() aggregates operating characteristics analysis output supplied boundaries.

title: Investigating the Freidlin and Korn strong null scenario
text: Four methods compared. unweighted log-rank test weights events equally. Fleming-Harrington FH(0,1) weighted log-rank test -weights late events test directly affected concern raised . MaxCombo test takes significant set Fleming-Harrington components, inherits behavior late-emphasis members. modestly-weighted log-rank test Magirr Burman (2019) also -weights late events, caps weight single region can dominate, intended prevent exactly kind spurious rejection issue . single simulated data set reused across methods. weighted log-rank tests occupy logrank.* columns, obtained separate analysis_fast calls; analysis calendar-time cut 5 years testing one-sided direction experimental benefit.
code: NSIM    <- 2000L     # raise (e.g. 5000) for tighter Monte Carlo error N_TOTAL <- 2000L ALPHA   <- 0.025 SIDE    <- 1L T_STAR  <- 0.5       # modestly-weighted log-rank delay (years) SEED    <- 20240601L  run_one_scenario <- function(scn) {   dataset <- simdata_fast(     nsim     = NSIM,     n        = rep(N_TOTAL %/% 2L, 2L),     a.time   = c(0, 1e-4),       # near-instantaneous enrollment     a.prop   = 1,     e.hazard = list(scn$haz_c, scn$haz_e),     e.time   = e_time,     seed     = SEED   )    res_lr   <- analysis_fast(dataset, control = 1, time.looks = cut,                             stat = "logrank", side = SIDE)   res_fh01 <- analysis_fast(dataset, control = 1, time.looks = cut,                             stat = "logrank", weight = "fh",                             rho = 0, gamma = 1, side = SIDE)   res_mb   <- analysis_fast(dataset, control = 1, time.looks = cut,                             stat = "logrank", weight = "mwlrt",                             t_star = T_STAR, side = SIDE)   res_mc   <- analysis_fast(dataset, control = 1, time.looks = cut,                             stat = "maxcombo",                             mc.rho = c(0, 0, 1, 1), mc.gamma = c(0, 1, 0, 1),                             side = SIDE)    reject_in_favor <- function(res, pcol) {     ok <- res$reached     mean(res[[pcol]][ok] <= ALPHA, na.rm = TRUE)   }   c("Log-rank"          = reject_in_favor(res_lr,   "logrank.p"),     "FH(0,1)"           = reject_in_favor(res_fh01, "logrank.p"),     "Modestly weighted" = reject_in_favor(res_mb,   "logrank.p"),     "MaxCombo"          = reject_in_favor(res_mc,   "maxcombo.p")) }

what: Step 3: assemble the per-look effect estimates and the two indicators
title: MRCT with complex survival analysis design
text: lines base R reshape long output one row per (sim, look) carrying overall log-rank Z, overall hazard ratio, region-1 hazard ratio, Method 1 Method 2 indicators. region events yet early cut (real possibility late-starting region), hazard ratio NA; undemonstrated criterion treated met.
code: ov  <- res[res$population == "overall", ] key <- function(d) paste(d$sim, d$look)  # Region-1 hazard ratio aligned to the overall rows r1  <- res[res$population == "subgroup_1", ] ov$HR.1 <- r1$cox.hr[match(key(ov), key(r1))]  # Method 2: all regional hazard ratios below 1 at this (sim, look) reg <- res[res$population != "overall", ] all_below_1 <- tapply(reg$cox.hr, key(reg), function(h) all(h < 1)) ov$M2 <- as.integer(all_below_1[key(ov)])  # Method 1: region 1 retains at least a fraction PI of the overall effect ov$M1 <- as.integer((1 - ov$HR.1) > PI * (1 - ov$cox.hr))  # Undemonstrated criteria (NA hazard ratio) count as not met ov$M1[is.na(ov$M1)] <- 0L ov$M2[is.na(ov$M2)] <- 0L

title: MRCT with complex survival analysis design
text: looks arranged simulation--look matrix boundaries applied sequence. trial stops first boundary crossing; boundaries crossed look, efficacy takes precedence.
code: sims  <- sort(unique(ov$sim));  nsim <- length(sims) looks <- sort(unique(ov$look)); nlk  <- length(looks) cell  <- cbind(match(ov$sim, sims), match(ov$look, looks)) mk    <- function(v) { m <- matrix(NA_real_, nsim, nlk); m[cell] <- v; m }  Zm   <- mk(ov$logrank.z) M1m  <- mk(ov$M1) M2m  <- mk(ov$M2) Cutm <- mk(ov$cutoff)  effB <- matrix(eff.bound, nsim, nlk, byrow = TRUE) futB <- matrix(fut.bound, nsim, nlk, byrow = TRUE) eff_cross <- !is.na(effB) & Zm <= effB fut_cross <- !is.na(futB) & Zm >= futB stop_any  <- eff_cross | fut_cross  first_stop <- apply(stop_any, 1L, function(r) {   w <- which(r); if (length(w)) w[1L] else nlk }) reject <- eff_cross[cbind(seq_len(nsim), first_stop)]

code: # survfit_fast assumes time-sorted input; the C++ core groups tied times in the # survival::survfit risk-set convention, so the data only need ordering by time. ord  <- order(gbsg$rfstime) fast <- survfit_fast(gbsg$rfstime[ord], gbsg$status[ord],                      t_eval = 1000, conf.type = "log-log")  fit  <- survfit(Surv(rfstime, status) ~ 1, data = gbsg) ref  <- summary(fit, times = 1000)  data.frame(   quantity = c("survival", "std.err"),   fast     = c(unclass(fast)["surv"], unclass(fast)["std.err"]),   survival = c(ref$surv, ref$std.err),   row.names = NULL ) #>   quantity       fast   survival #> 1 survival 0.65775040 0.65775040 #> 2  std.err 0.01905146 0.01905146

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: wmst_fast() integrates Kaplan-Meier curve lower upper window limit, generalizing restricted mean survival time. CRAN dedicated WMST package, validate per-group windowed area directly Kaplan-Meier step-function integral computed survfit() window. full comparison survWMST package, distributed GitHub, provided tools/compare_wmst_survwmst.R.
code: tau1 <- 200 tau2 <- 1000  fast_wm <- wmst_fast(gbsg$rfstime, gbsg$status, gbsg$hormon,                      control = 0, side = 1, tau1 = tau1, tau2 = tau2)  # Integral of the Kaplan-Meier step function over [lo, hi] for one group. km_window_area <- function(gi, lo, hi) {   sf <- survfit(Surv(rfstime, status) ~ 1, data = gbsg[gbsg$hormon == gi, ])   tk <- c(0, sf$time)   sk <- c(1, sf$surv)   brk  <- sort(unique(c(lo, hi, sf$time[sf$time > lo & sf$time < hi])))   area <- 0   for (b in seq_len(length(brk) - 1L)) {     u  <- brk[b]     su <- sk[max(which(tk <= u))]     area <- area + su * (brk[b + 1L] - u)   }   area }  data.frame(   group    = c("control", "treatment"),   fast     = unclass(fast_wm)[c("wmst.control", "wmst.treatment")],   survfit  = c(km_window_area(0, tau1, tau2), km_window_area(1, tau1, tau2)),   row.names = NULL ) #>       group     fast  survfit #> 1   control 628.0064 628.0064 #> 2 treatment 678.3393 678.3393

code: tmax <- max(gbsg$rfstime)  fast_wk <- wkm_fast(gbsg$rfstime, gbsg$status, gbsg$hormon,                     control = 0, side = 1, weight = "constant")  ref_rm  <- rmst_fast(gbsg$rfstime, gbsg$status, gbsg$hormon,                      control = 0, tau = tmax, side = 1)  c(wkm.constant = unclass(fast_wk)["wdiff"], rmst = unclass(ref_rm)["diff"]) #> wkm.constant.wdiff          rmst.diff  #>           256.7207           256.7207

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: ahr_fast() computes Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio two groups restricted interval. reference implementation ahrKM() AHR package, package archived CRAN, check point estimates direct survival-based computation: group’s Kaplan-Meier curve survfit(), integrated form group shares total hazard. point estimates match survival-based reference numerical precision. average hazard ratio additionally cross-checked archived AHR package, reference implementation used Dormuth et al. (2024): two group shares, average hazard ratio, variances, test statistics agree within order 1e-15. external comparison reproducible tools/compare_ahr_ahrKM.R script shipped package sources.
code: tau <- 1000  fast_ahr <- ahr_fast(gbsg$rfstime, gbsg$status, gbsg$hormon,                      control = 0, tau = tau, side = 1)  g  <- gbsg$hormon ev <- c(gbsg$rfstime[g == 0 & gbsg$status == 1],         gbsg$rfstime[g == 1 & gbsg$status == 1]) grid <- sort(unique(c(0, ev[ev <= tau], tau)))  km_on_grid <- function(gi) {   sf <- survfit(Surv(rfstime, status) ~ 1, data = gbsg[g == gi, ])   approxfun(sf$time, sf$surv, method = "constant",             yleft = 1, rule = 2, f = 0)(grid) }  S0  <- km_on_grid(0) S1  <- km_on_grid(1) m   <- length(grid) dS0 <- S0 - c(1, S0[-m]) GL  <- S0[m] * S1[m] ref_theta_ctrl <- -sum(S1 * dS0) / (1 - GL)  data.frame(   quantity  = c("theta (control)", "theta (treatment)", "AHR"),   fast      = c(fast_ahr$theta[[1]], fast_ahr$theta[[2]], fast_ahr$ahr),   reference = c(ref_theta_ctrl, 1 - ref_theta_ctrl,                 (1 - ref_theta_ctrl) / ref_theta_ctrl),   row.names = NULL ) #>            quantity      fast reference #> 1   theta (control) 0.5974108 0.5974108 #> 2 theta (treatment) 0.4025892 0.4025892 #> 3               AHR 0.6738899 0.6738899

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: maxcombo_fast() computes max-combo test, extreme set Fleming-Harrington weighted log-rank statistics. nph package provides logrank.maxtest(), whose default weight set FH(0, 0), FH(0, 1), FH(1, 0). request three weights maxcombo_fast() compare component Z-scores. individual Z-scores kept z attribute, signed negative value indicates benefit treatment group package convention. nph orients contrast opposite direction, signs mirrored, compare absolute values. component statistics agree absolute value. final max-combo statistic largest , one-sided test extreme component Z. p-values two packages close identical, since use different numerical methods joint distribution (multivariate normal integral , Bonferroni-style combination nph).
code: rho   <- c(0, 0, 1) gamma <- c(0, 1, 0)  fast_mc <- maxcombo_fast(gbsg$rfstime, gbsg$status, gbsg$hormon,                          control = 0, side = 1, rho = rho, gamma = gamma)  nph_mc <- nph::logrank.maxtest(gbsg$rfstime, gbsg$status, gbsg$hormon)  data.frame(   weight = c("FH(0,0)", "FH(0,1)", "FH(1,0)"),   fast   = abs(attr(fast_mc, "z")),   nph    = abs(nph_mc$tests$z),   row.names = NULL ) #>    weight     fast      nph #> 1 FH(0,0) 2.926565 2.926565 #> 2 FH(0,1) 2.260677 2.260677 #> 3 FH(1,0) 2.951913 2.951913

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: rmw_fast() computes robust modestly-weighted (rMW) test Magirr Öhrn, maximum standard log-rank statistic single modestly-weighted log-rank statistic. component weighted log-rank test, check two component Z-scores nphRCT, implementation used method’s authors. modestly-weighted component uses survival-threshold parameterization, s_star = 1 recovers standard log-rank test s_star = 0.5 gives modestly-weighted component. max-combo test package orients negative Z toward treatment benefit nphRCT uses opposite sign, compare absolute values. component statistics agree absolute value. null correlation two components, reported corr attribute, matches covariance computed authors’ find_cor routine numerical precision. combined statistic one-sided p-value follow bivariate normal distribution two components, evaluated mvtnorm::pmvnorm. POPLAR overall-survival data analyzed original article, rmw_fast() reproduces published one-sided p-values 0.0028 log-rank test, 0.0009 modestly-weighted test, 0.0012 rMW test, component correlation 0.97.
code: gbsg_df <- data.frame(   time  = gbsg$rfstime,   event = gbsg$status,   arm   = factor(ifelse(gbsg$hormon == 0, "control", "experimental"),                  levels = c("control", "experimental")) )  fit_rmw <- rmw_fast(gbsg_df$time, gbsg_df$event, gbsg_df$arm,                     control = "control", side = 1, s_star = 0.5)  z_lr_nph <- nphRCT::wlrt(Surv(time, event) ~ arm, data = gbsg_df,                          method = "mw", s_star = 1)$z z_mw_nph <- nphRCT::wlrt(Surv(time, event) ~ arm, data = gbsg_df,                          method = "mw", s_star = 0.5)$z  data.frame(   component = c("log-rank (s_star = 1)", "modestly-weighted (s_star = 0.5)"),   fast      = abs(attr(fit_rmw, "z")),   nphRCT    = abs(c(z_lr_nph, z_mw_nph)),   row.names = NULL ) #>                          component     fast   nphRCT #> 1            log-rank (s_star = 1) 2.926565 2.926565 #> 2 modestly-weighted (s_star = 0.5) 2.773749 2.773749

what: Fast Survival Analysis and Simulation for Clinical Trials
title: Fast Survival Analysis and Simulation for Clinical Trials
text: FastSurvival provides fast alternatives standard survival analysis functions survival package, together simulation layer designing evaluating time--event trials. Every function designed repeated evaluation inside large simulation loops — adaptive sample-size re-estimation, probability--success calculations, regional consistency evaluation multi-regional trials — standard iterative object-building overhead survfit(), survdiff(), coxph() becomes computational bottleneck. Core computations implemented C++ via Rcpp maximum performance. full documentation site, including function references articles, available https://gosukehommaEX.github.io/FastSurvival/.

code: library(FastSurvival) library(survival)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # survfit_fast(): Kaplan-Meier at a single time point # ---------------------------------------------------------------- ord <- order(ovarian$futime) t_s <- ovarian$futime[ord] e_s <- ovarian$fustat[ord]  survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 500, conf.type = "log")  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # survdiff_fast(): log-rank test # ---------------------------------------------------------------- survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,               control = 1, side = 2)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # coxph_fast(): hazard ratio via the Pike-Halley Estimator (closed-form) # ---------------------------------------------------------------- coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # rmst_fast(): restricted mean survival time (two-group) # ---------------------------------------------------------------- rmst_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,           control = 1, tau = 500)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # wmst_fast(): window mean survival time over [100, 500] # ---------------------------------------------------------------- wmst_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,           control = 1, tau1 = 100, tau2 = 500)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # medsurv_fast(): median survival time (two-group) # ---------------------------------------------------------------- medsurv_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # wkm_fast(): weighted Kaplan-Meier (Pepe-Fleming) test # ---------------------------------------------------------------- wkm_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # simdata_fast(): clinical trial simulation # ----------------------------------------------------------------  # Two-group trial, simple exponential, no dropout df <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = 1000,   n        = c(100, 100),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 200 / 12,   e.median = list(18, 24),   seed     = 1 ) head(df)  # Two-group trial, piecewise exponential (delayed treatment effect) df2 <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = 1000,   n        = c(100, 100),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 200 / 12,   e.hazard = list(c(0.08, 0.08), c(0.08, 0.04)),   e.time   = c(0, 6, Inf),   seed     = 2 )  # Two correlated endpoints (e.g. PFS and OS) from an illness-death model: # an intermediate event (h01) and a terminal event (h02). With the post-event # hazard defaulting to h02 this is the Fleischer maximal-independence model. df3 <- simdata_fast(   nsim       = 1000,   n          = c(100, 100),   a.time     = c(0, 12),   a.rate     = 200 / 12,   h01.median = list(6, 9),     # intermediate event (e.g. progression)   h02.median = list(14, 18),   # terminal event (e.g. death)   seed       = 3 )

title: Fast Survival Analysis and Simulation for Clinical Trials
text: typical simulation workflow combines simulation analysis layers. simdata_fast() generates data , analysis_fast() performs looks computes statistics every simulated trial, simsummary_fast() aggregates operating characteristics supplied boundaries: estimation functions can also used directly inside loop. returned object internally numeric vector, stripping class unclass() per-simulation results can combined rbind() ordinary numeric matrix:
code: library(FastSurvival)  # 1. Simulate a two-group trial with a delayed treatment effect df <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = 1000,   n        = c(350, 350),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 700 / 12,   e.hazard = list(c(0.08, 0.08), c(0.08, 0.04)),   e.time   = c(0, 6, Inf),   d.hazard = list(0.01, 0.01),   seed     = 42 )  # 2. Analyze at two interim looks defined by target event counts res <- analysis_fast(   df, control = 1,   event.looks = c(300, 450),   stat = "logrank", side = 1 )  # 3. Summarize operating characteristics against efficacy boundaries #    (nominal levels from a group-sequential design, e.g. gsDesign) oc <- simsummary_fast(   res,   p.col = "logrank.p",   alpha = c(0.0006, 0.0238) ) oc results <- vector("list", 1000L) for (s in seq_len(1000L)) {   d <- df[df$sim == s, ]   results[[s]] <- coxph_fast(d$tte, d$event, d$group, control = 1) }  hr_mat <- do.call(rbind, lapply(results, unclass)) colMeans(hr_mat)

code: set.seed(1) n <- 200 time1 <- rexp(n, 0.1) time2 <- rexp(n, 0.18) cens <- rexp(2 * n, 0.05) obs <- pmin(c(time1, time2), cens) event <- as.integer(c(time1, time2) <= cens) group <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n) ahr_fast(obs, event, group, control = 0, tau = 8) #> Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio (two-group) #>  #>   tau = 8,  control = 0 #>   alternative = two.sided #>  #>            theta   n #> control   0.3079 200 #> treatment 0.6921 200 #>  #>                                             Est. lower 95% upper 95%     z #> average hazard ratio (treatment / control) 2.248     1.699     2.973 6.317 #>                                            Pr(>|z|)     #> average hazard ratio (treatment / control) 2.66e-10 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>   (log scale: z = 5.676, p = 1.379e-08)

title: Fast Closed-Form Hazard Ratio Estimation via the Pike-Halley Estimator — coxph_fast
text: Let t_k (k = 1, ..., K) denote distinct observed event times pooled sample. t_k, let n_T_k n_C_k numbers risk treatment control groups just t_k, let O_T_k O_C_k numbers events group, n_k = n_T_k + n_C_k O_k = O_T_k + O_C_k. Define E_T = sum n_T_k O_k / n_k E_C = sum n_C_k O_k / n_k log-rank expected event totals. Pike-Halley Estimator obtained three steps. First, Pike anchor computed theta_0 = (O_T E_C) / (O_C E_T). Second, score U_0, observed information I_0, third-order curvature term J_0 Breslow partial likelihood evaluated eta_0 = log(theta_0): p_k = n_T_k theta_0 / (n_C_k + n_T_k theta_0) U_0 = sum (O_T_k - O_k p_k) I_0 = sum O_k p_k (1 - p_k) J_0 = sum O_k p_k (1 - p_k) (1 - 2 p_k) Third, closed-form Halley correction applied: delta_hat = U_0 / I_0 - J_0 U_0^2 / (2 I_0^3) theta_hat = theta_0 exp(delta_hat) residual error satisfies |theta_hat - theta_Cox| = O_p(n^{-3/2}), three orders magnitude faster O_p(n^{-1/2}) rate Peto Pike, per-call cost approximately thirty times lower iterative Cox solver (Homma, 2025). Wald standard error log scale SE = 1 / sqrt(I_0), I_0 observed information evaluated Pike anchor. quantity used Wald confidence interval reported summary(coxph(...)), based observed information maximum likelihood estimate. Pike anchor lies within O_p(n^{-1/2}) Cox maximum likelihood estimate, difference I_0 information maximum likelihood estimate negligible purpose interval construction. C++ core (pihe_core) accepts pooled sorted data together integer group indicator performs group splitting, -risk counting, per-distinct-event-time accumulation single left--right scan. eliminates rev(cumsum(rev(...))), tapply(), (), diff(), group-split vector copies present pure-R version. returned object class "coxph_fast" named numeric vector length 5. print() method formats result similarly summary(coxph(...)).

code: library(survival)  # Compare coxph_fast with coxph on the ovarian dataset. # coxph() treats rx as numeric with rx=1 as the reference (control), # so set control = 1 for a consistent comparison. fit_fast <- coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1) fit_fast #> Pike-Halley estimator for the hazard ratio (two-group) #>  #>   control = 1 #>   alternative = two.sided #>  #> Coefficients: #>          coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|) #> group -0.5964    0.5508   0.5868 -1.016     0.31 #>  #> Hazard ratio and 95% Wald confidence interval: #>       exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> group    0.5508     1.8155    0.1744    1.7399  fit_cox <- summary(coxph(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx, data = ovarian)) cat("coxph_fast HR :", fit_fast["exp(coef)"], "\n") #> coxph_fast HR : 0.5508019  cat("coxph      HR :", fit_cox$coefficients[, "exp(coef)"], "\n") #> coxph      HR : 0.5508019   # presorted = TRUE: sort once outside, reuse inside a loop ord <- order(ovarian$futime) coxph_fast(ovarian$futime[ord], ovarian$fustat[ord], ovarian$rx[ord],            control = 1, presorted = TRUE) #> Pike-Halley estimator for the hazard ratio (two-group) #>  #>   control = 1 #>   alternative = two.sided #>  #> Coefficients: #>          coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|) #> group -0.5964    0.5508   0.5868 -1.016     0.31 #>  #> Hazard ratio and 95% Wald confidence interval: #>       exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> group    0.5508     1.8155    0.1744    1.7399  # \donttest{ library(microbenchmark) microbenchmark(   coxph_fast = coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, 2),   coxph      = coxph(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx, data = ovarian),   times = 1000 ) #> Unit: microseconds #>        expr    min      lq      mean  median      uq     max neval cld #>  coxph_fast   23.0   52.05   82.9365   74.05  104.55   511.1  1000  a  #>       coxph 1034.9 1721.55 2253.7761 2144.65 2555.55 15128.3  1000   b # }

C++ backend locates evaluation cutoff via binary search     accumulates Kaplan-Meier product Greenwood sum single     scan event positions , without constructing intermediate     vectors, making approximately 50 times faster survfit()     plus summary() repeated single-time-point evaluations. survdiff_fast Log-rank test statistic (one-sided     Z-score two-sided chi-square) two-group survival data. C++     backend uses two-pointer merge scan pooled sorted vectors,     eliminating rank(), tabulate(),     rev(cumsum(rev(...))) overhead standard implementation,     making approximately 40 times faster survdiff(). coxph_fast Closed-form hazard ratio estimator     via Pike-Halley Estimator method, Wald confidence interval.     Pike-Halley Estimator anchors Pike estimate applies     single analytic Halley correction Cox partial likelihood score,     achieving residual error order O_p(n^{-3/2}) relative     Cox maximum likelihood estimate. C++ backend performs group     splitting, -risk counting, per-distinct-event-time accumulation     single pass, making approximately 30 times faster     coxph(). simdata_fast Clinical trial data simulator     one- two-group time--event trials. Supports piecewise uniform     accrual, simple piecewise exponential survival dropout times.     C++ backends handle piecewise sampling two-group interleaving,     random number generation uses dqrng, making     approximately 4 times faster equivalent pure-R     implementation.

what: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
title: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
text: Internal C++ function computes restricted mean survival time (RMST) Greenwood-type variance single group horizon tau. single left--right scan sorted data accumulates Kaplan-Meier product area survival step function, storing one summary per distinct event time (cumulative area left time Greenwood increment). second short pass saved summaries forms variance sum squared right-side areas weighted Greenwood increments. Accepts event vector numeric (double) vector avoid .integer() copy R. intended called directly users; use rmst_fast() instead.

title: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
code: rmst_core(t_sorted, e_sorted, tau)

title: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
text: t_sorted numeric vector follow-times sorted ascending order. e_sorted numeric vector event indicators (1 = event, 0 = censored), aligned t_sorted. Accepted numeric avoid integer-conversion copy R side. tau single numeric value specifying restriction horizon.

title: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
text: numeric vector length 2: c(rmst, var_rmst),   rmst restricted mean survival time var_rmst   variance, SE[RMST] = sqrt(var_rmst). Returns   c(tau, 0.0) events occur tau.

what: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
text: Computes restricted mean survival time (RMST) horizon tau Kaplan-Meier estimator. single group (default), returns RMST Greenwood-type standard error Wald confidence interval. group supplied, additionally returns two-group contrasts: RMST difference (treatment minus control) RMST ratio (treatment control), standard error, confidence interval, two-sided test. C++ backend integrates survival step function single scan reused per group, function suitable simulation loops presorted = TRUE.

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
code: rmst_fast(   time,   event,   group = NULL,   control = NULL,   side = 2,   conf.level = 0.95,   tau,   presorted = FALSE )

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
text: time numeric vector event censoring times. event integer numeric vector event indicators (1 = event, 0 = censored), aligned time. group optional vector group labels aligned time. NULL (default), single-group RMST computed. supplied, two-group contrasts returned control must given. control scalar value indicating level group represents control group. Required group supplied. side 1 one-sided test direction treatment benefit (longer restricted mean survival treatment group) 2 two-sided test (default 2). Applies two-group contrasts; confidence intervals always two-sided conf.level. conf.level single numeric value (0, 1) specifying confidence level. Defaults 0.95. tau single positive numeric value specifying restriction horizon. presorted logical value. TRUE, inputs assumed sorted ascending order time. FALSE (default), sorting handled internally.

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
text: object class "rmst_fast", named numeric vector.   single-group mode length 4 elements rmst,   std.err, lower, upper. two-group mode   contains per-group RMST (rmst.ctrl, rmst.trt),   difference contrast (diff, se.diff, diff.lower,   diff.upper, z.diff, p.diff), ratio contrast   (ratio, ratio.lower, ratio.upper, z.ratio,   p.ratio). restriction horizon, confidence level, test side   stored attributes tau, conf.level, side;   two-group mode control label also stored. Returns   NA_real_ values (still class "rmst_fast") n   zero single-group mode.

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
text: RMST area Kaplan-Meier curve 0 tau: RMST(tau) = integral [0, tau] S(u) du. variance follows Greenwood-type estimator Var[RMST] = sum_{t_i <= tau} A_i^2 d_i / (n_i (n_i - d_i)), A_i = integral [t_i, tau] S(u) du area right event time t_i. matches restricted mean reported survfit survRM2 package. group supplied, treatment group level group equal control. Writing treatment control RMST r1 r0 variances v1 v0, difference contrast diff = r1 - r0 Var[diff] = v1 + v0, using independence two groups. ratio contrast formed log scale delta method, Var[log(r1 / r0)] = v1 / r1^2 + v0 / r0^2, confidence interval exponentiated back ratio scale. match unadjusted contrasts reported survRM2. presorted = TRUE, input vectors assumed sorted ascending order time; splitting groups preserves ascending order within group, re-sorting performed. presorted = FALSE (default), sorting handled internally. simulation loops data generated sorted order, presorted = TRUE avoids one O(n log n) pass.

code: set.seed(42) t_raw <- rexp(100, rate = 1 / 10) e_raw <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.7)  # Single-group RMST rmst_fast(t_raw, e_raw, tau = 10) #> Restricted mean survival time (single-group) #>  #>   tau = 10 #>  #>            RMST std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> tau = 10 7.0341  0.3452    6.3575    7.7107  # Single-group, pre-sorted (sort once, reuse in a loop) ord <- order(t_raw) rmst_fast(t_raw[ord], e_raw[ord], tau = 10, presorted = TRUE) #> Restricted mean survival time (single-group) #>  #>   tau = 10 #>  #>            RMST std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> tau = 10 7.0341  0.3452    6.3575    7.7107  # Two-group comparison (difference and ratio) set.seed(7) n <- 200 time  <- c(rexp(n, 0.10), rexp(n, 0.07)) event <- rbinom(2 * n, 1, 0.8) group <- rep(0:1, each = n) rmst_fast(time, event, group = group, control = 0, tau = 10) #> Restricted mean survival time (two-group) #>  #>   tau = 10,  control = 0 #>   alternative = two.sided #>  #>             RMST #> control   6.7572 #> treatment 7.8384 #>  #>                                    Est. lower 95% upper 95%     z Pr(>|z|)    #> difference (treatment - control) 1.0813    0.4094    1.7531 3.154  0.00161 ** #> ratio (treatment / control)      1.1600    1.0572    1.2729 3.133  0.00173 ** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1  # \donttest{ # Validation against survRM2 if (requireNamespace("survRM2", quietly = TRUE)) {   survRM2::rmst2(time, event, group, tau = 10)$unadjusted.result } #>                           Est. lower .95 upper .95           p #> RMST (arm=1)-(arm=0) 1.0812605 0.4094144 1.7531066 0.001608610 #> RMST (arm=1)/(arm=0) 1.1600165 1.0571618 1.2728783 0.001727894 #> RMTL (arm=1)/(arm=0) 0.6665677 0.5119289 0.8679185 0.002596875 # }

") #> survdiff_fast chi-square: 1.06274  cat("survdiff      chi-square:", chisq_ref,       "
") #> survdiff      chi-square: 1.06274   # One-sided test (Z-score) survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, 2, side = 1) #> Log-rank test (two-group) #>  #>   N = 26,  control = 2 #>  #>           Observed Expected (O-E)^2/E (O-E)^2/V #> control          5   6.7665    0.4612    1.0627 #> treatment        7   5.2335    0.5962    1.0627 #>  #>  Z = 1.031,  one-sided p-value = 0.8487   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1  # presorted = TRUE: sort once outside, reuse inside a loop ord <- order(ovarian$futime) survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime[ord], ovarian$fustat[ord], ovarian$rx[ord],               control = 2, side = 2, presorted = TRUE) #> Log-rank test (two-group) #>  #>   N = 26,  control = 2 #>  #>           Observed Expected (O-E)^2/E (O-E)^2/V #> control          5   6.7665    0.4612    1.0627 #> treatment        7   5.2335    0.5962    1.0627 #>  #>  Chi-square = 1.063 on 1 df,  p-value = 0.3026   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1  # Stratified log-rank test: compare with survdiff + strata() fit_str <- survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, 2,                          side = 2, strata = ovarian$resid.ds) chisq_str <- survdiff(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx + strata(resid.ds),                       data = ovarian)$chisq cat("stratified survdiff_fast:", as.numeric(fit_str), "

title: Fast Kaplan-Meier Survival Probability at a Specified Time Point — survfit_fast
text: Kaplan-Meier estimate time t_eval defined product-limit estimator evaluated largest observed event time less equal t_eval. t_eval smaller first observed event time, S(t) = 1 standard error zero. standard error estimated Greenwood's formula: SE[S(t)] = S(t) * sqrt(sum_{t_i <= t, d_i > 0} d_i / (n_i * (n_i - d_i))) d_i number events n_i number risk time t_i. output field std.err follows convention survfit, reports SE[S(t)] / S(t) (.e., standard error log scale) conf.type != "plain", SE[S(t)] conf.type = "plain". function always returns SE[S(t)] (standard error survival scale). S(t_eval) = 0 (subjects experienced event t_eval), standard error zero confidence interval collapses [0, 0], consistent survfit. presorted = TRUE (default), t_sorted e_sorted assumed sorted ascending order time. presorted = FALSE, vectors sorted internally computation. Three confidence interval types supported via conf.type: "plain": Linear interval survival scale,     S(t) +/- z * SE. bounds clipped [0, 1]. "log": Interval log scale (default     survfit),     S(t) * exp(+/- z * SE / S(t)). "log-log": Interval complementary log-log scale,     S(t)^exp(+/- z * SE / (S(t) * log(S(t)))). returned object class "survfit_fast" named numeric vector length 4 evaluation time t_eval, confidence level conf.level, confidence interval type conf.type stored attributes. print() method formats result similarly print(summary(survival::survfit(...))).

code: set.seed(42) t_raw <- rexp(100, rate = 1 / 10) e_raw <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.7)  # presorted = TRUE (default): sort once outside, reuse inside a loop ord <- order(t_raw) t_s <- t_raw[ord] e_s <- e_raw[ord] survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 10, conf.type = "plain") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543    0.3679    0.5809 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: plain survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 10, conf.type = "log") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543     0.379    0.5938 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 10, conf.type = "log-log") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543    0.3651    0.5759 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log-log  # presorted = FALSE: sort internally, convenient for one-off calls survfit_fast(t_raw, e_raw, t_eval = 10, presorted = FALSE) #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543     0.379    0.5938 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log  # Validation against survival::survfit library(survival) fit <- survfit(Surv(t_raw, e_raw) ~ 1, conf.type = "plain") summary(fit, times = 10) #> Call: survfit(formula = Surv(t_raw, e_raw) ~ 1, conf.type = "plain") #>  #>  time n.risk n.event survival std.err lower 95% CI upper 95% CI #>    10     37      46    0.474  0.0543        0.368        0.581

title: FastSurvival 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-27 Initial release. Core computations implemented C++ via Rcpp use inside large simulation loops. survfit_fast(): single-time-point Kaplan-Meier estimator Greenwood standard error plain / log / log-log confidence intervals. C++ backend locates evaluation cutoff via binary search accumulates Kaplan-Meier product Greenwood variance sum single scan event positions. Returns object class "survfit_fast" print() method. survdiff_fast(): log-rank test returning one-sided Z-score two-sided chi-square statistic. C++ backend uses two-pointer merge scan pooled sorted vectors, eliminating rank construction, tabulate(), reverse cumulative sum operations standard implementation. Returns object class "survdiff_fast" print() method. coxph_fast(): closed-form hazard ratio estimator via Pike-Halley Estimator method Wald confidence interval. C++ backend performs group splitting, -risk counting, per-distinct-event-time accumulation single pass. Returns object class "coxph_fast" print() method. simdata_fast(): clinical trial data simulator supporting one- two-group designs, piecewise uniform accrual, simple piecewise exponential survival dropout times. C++ backends handle piecewise sampling two-group interleaving, random number generation uses dqrng.
